examples.q: A force of 20 N is applied perpendicular to a wrench 0.3 m from the pivot. What is the torque produced?
examples.solution:
Torque is the rotational effect of a force.
It depends on the force AND where it is applied.

Formula:
    τ = F × d

where  τ = torque             (N·m)
       F = applied force      (N)
       d = moment arm (lever arm) — the perpendicular
           distance from the pivot to the line of force (m)

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    F = 20 N
    d = 0.3 m

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    τ = 20 × 0.3
    τ = 6 N·m

Answer:  τ = 6 N·m

Note: The force is already perpendicular,
so no angle adjustment is needed.

examples.q: A 50 N force is applied at 30° to a lever arm of 0.8 m. What is the torque?
examples.solution:
When the force is NOT perpendicular to the lever arm,
only the perpendicular component creates torque.

Formula:
    τ = F × d × sin θ

where  θ = angle between the force and the lever arm

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    F = 50 N
    d = 0.8 m
    θ = 30°

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    τ = 50 × 0.8 × sin 30°
    τ = 50 × 0.8 × 0.5
    τ = 20 N·m

Answer:  τ = 20 N·m

Note: Maximum torque occurs at θ = 90°
(force fully perpendicular to lever arm).

examples.q: A seesaw is balanced. A 40 N weight sits 2 m from the pivot on the left. Where must a 80 N weight be placed on the right to keep it balanced?
examples.solution:
For a seesaw to be balanced (in rotational equilibrium),
the total clockwise torque must equal the total
counterclockwise torque:

    τ_left = τ_right
    F₁ × d₁ = F₂ × d₂

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    F₁ = 40 N,  d₁ = 2 m  (left side)
    F₂ = 80 N,  d₂ = ?    (right side)

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    40 × 2 = 80 × d₂
    80 = 80 × d₂
    d₂ = 80 / 80
    d₂ = 1 m

Answer:  The 80 N weight must be placed  1 m
from the pivot on the right side.

questions.q: A 15 N force is applied perpendicular to a lever 0.5 m from the pivot. What is the torque?
questions.given:
Formula:  τ = F × d
Force is perpendicular — no angle needed.

questions.q: What is the unit of torque?
questions.given:
Torque = Force × distance.
Force is in Newtons (N); distance is in meters (m).
questions.sol: Torque = F × d = N × m = N·m (Newton-meter).

questions.q: A force of 30 N is applied at 90° to a lever arm of 0.6 m. What is the torque?
questions.given:
Formula:  τ = F × d × sin θ
sin 90° = 1

questions.q: A 60 N force is applied at 0° to a lever arm (parallel to it). What is the torque produced?
questions.given:
Formula:  τ = F × d × sin θ
sin 0° = 0
questions.sol: τ = F × d × sin 0° = F × d × 0 = 0 N·m. A force parallel to the lever arm produces no torque.

questions.q: At what angle should a force be applied to a lever arm to produce maximum torque?
questions.given:
Formula:  τ = F × d × sin θ
Consider which angle maximizes sin θ.
questions.sol: sin 90° = 1, which is the maximum value of sin θ. Maximum torque is produced when force is perpendicular to the lever arm.

questions.q: A torque of 24 N·m is produced by a perpendicular force applied 0.4 m from the pivot. What is the force?
questions.given:
Rearrange:  τ = F × d
→  F = τ / d

questions.q: A 100 N weight sits 3 m from the pivot on one side of a seesaw. What force must be applied 5 m from the pivot on the other side to balance it?
questions.given:
Rotational equilibrium:  F₁ × d₁ = F₂ × d₂
Solve for F₂.

questions.q: Which of the following changes would NOT increase the torque on a bolt being turned by a wrench?
questions.given:
τ = F × d × sin θ
Consider each factor that affects torque.
opts: Applying the force closer to the pivot
Applying the force at 90° to the wrench
questions.sol: Applying force closer to the pivot reduces the moment arm (d), which decreases torque. All other options increase torque.

questions.q: A force of 40 N is applied at 30° to a 1.5 m lever arm. What is the torque?
questions.given:
Formula:  τ = F × d × sin θ
sin 30° = 0.5

questions.q: Two forces act on opposite sides of a pivot. Force A = 25 N at 2 m. Force B = 50 N at 1 m. Is the system in rotational equilibrium?
questions.given:
Equilibrium requires:  τ_A = τ_B
Compare torques on each side.
questions.sol: τ_A = 25 × 2 = 50 N·m. τ_B = 50 × 1 = 50 N·m. Equal torques → rotational equilibrium.
